The likeliest culprit is sitting right in your log: 5.3 hours of sleep last night. A night that short reliably shows up as grogginess, low motivation, and heavier-feeling efforts the next day. Sleep is the only thing you're tracking at the moment — no heart-rate or recovery metrics — so I can't rule out other contributors like accumulated training or life stress, but short sleep alone is enough to explain feeling wrecked. For today, downshift rather than skip: easy movement, a walk or light spin, usually leaves you better off than either total rest or pushing a hard session. Then make tonight the fix — an earlier lights-out will do more for tomorrow than anything else you could change.

HRV is the variation in timing between your heartbeats, measured overnight. Counterintuitively, more variation is the good direction — it usually means your nervous system is in rest-and-recover mode rather than fight-or-flight. The number only means something against your own history, not anyone else's, and against yours today is a shade soft: 61 ms versus a 30-day average of 68.1 and a weekly average of 69. A single morning below baseline is normal noise; several in a row is what would actually matter. Worth noting your reading comes through a health platform that calculates HRV differently from some devices, one more reason to ignore other people's numbers. Check where tomorrow lands before reading anything into today's dip.

The higher-value move is dragging weeknight bedtime earlier so the weekend rescue becomes unnecessary. Keep this morning's numbers in mind as evidence of what a full night actually buys you.

HRV measures the small variations in timing between heartbeats while you sleep. Broadly, higher does mean better readiness — it signals your recovery systems are in charge — but only relative to your own baseline; comparing against other people is meaningless because normal ranges differ hugely from person to person. Against your own history, 81 ms is an excellent reading: your 30-day average is 72.4, so you're sitting well above it, and the supporting cast agrees — resting heart rate at 51 bpm versus a usual 54, recovery at 82%. One caution: a sudden extreme HRV spike right after unusually hard stress can occasionally be noise rather than fitness. Today's isn't extreme — it's simply a good day, and a fine one for quality training.

HRV is heart rate variability — the tiny differences in timing between one heartbeat and the next. Counterintuitively, more variability is generally the good sign: when your nervous system is rested and balanced, beat-to-beat timing fluctuates freely, and when you're run down from hard training, poor sleep, illness, or stress, the rhythm gets more metronome-like and HRV drops. That's why trackers use it as a recovery signal. Two honest caveats: it's only meaningful against your own baseline, since normal values vary hugely between people, and single days are noisy — trends over weeks are what count. In your case there's no HRV to look at, because manual logging has no sensor measuring it overnight; your log currently shows sleep, like last night's 6.7 h. If you ever want the metric itself, that takes a wearable.

HRV is heart rate variability: the tiny variations in time between one heartbeat and the next. Counterintuitively, more variability is generally the better sign; it suggests your nervous system is in a rested, adaptable state, while a drop below your personal norm often shows up after hard training, poor sleep, alcohol, or oncoming illness. It's useful precisely because it's compared against your own baseline rather than anyone else's. The reason it's missing from your stats is simple: measuring it requires a device sampling your heartbeat overnight, and you're currently logging everything manually, so there's no HRV, resting heart rate, or recovery score in your data. What you do have is sleep, like last night's 369 minutes, about 6.2 hours. If you want HRV, any tracker worn overnight will start building a baseline within a few weeks.

They're two different windows on the same recovery system. Resting heart rate, your 56 bpm today, is how fast your heart beats when you're fully at rest; lower relative to your own norm generally means fresher, and yours sits just under your 30-day average of 57.5. HRV, your 59 ms, is the variation in timing between individual beats, and here higher is the good direction, since a rested nervous system lets the gap between beats fluctuate more; you're right at your average of 58.5. The practical difference: resting heart rate is the slow, steady gauge that drifts up with illness or accumulated fatigue, while HRV is the twitchier day-to-day dial that reacts to a single hard session or bad night. Today both agree you're at baseline, which fits your recovery of 71%. When they disagree, trust the pair over either one alone.

HRV — heart rate variability — is the tiny variation in time between consecutive heartbeats. Counterintuitively, more variability is generally the good state: it suggests your nervous system is in rest-and-recover mode, while a drop below your own normal often follows hard training, poor sleep, alcohol, or oncoming illness. It's measured from continuous beat-to-beat heart data, usually overnight, which is why you don't see one: you're logging manually, and there's no sensor capturing those intervals. Your log does show last night's sleep at 456 minutes, about 7.6 h, which is a useful signal on its own. If HRV matters to you, a ring or chest strap would add it; otherwise sleep plus how you feel gets you most of the way.

Creatine sits in a completely different category from banned or prescription substances — it's legal, sold openly, and one of the most-studied supplements in sports science. The standard approach is 3 to 5 g of creatine monohydrate daily, with no loading phase needed. Expect roughly 1 kg of early water weight — that's muscle cells holding water, not fat.
